agent_raw:
**Review: Pharmacy Demand Forecasting Benchmark**

The reported 7% WAPE improvement is not a valid basis for replacing the incumbent model. Three fundamental problems undermine the comparison.

First, the data targets differ. The baseline WAPE was computed on the original raw sales series, while the new model was trained and evaluated on corrected data — unit-conversion errors fixed, stockout days backfilled using neighboring-store demand patterns. These are different target variables. A WAPE improvement against a different (and better-measured) target cannot be attributed to model quality.

Second, the forecast horizons are incompatible. The incumbent dashboard tracked one-day-ahead WAPE, while the new project reports seven-day aggregate WAPE. Seven-day aggregate accuracy is a different task: daily errors partially cancel across the week, mechanically reducing aggregate WAPE relative to daily WAPE. A model could show better seven-day aggregate WAPE than its one-day WAPE even with no change in underlying forecast quality.

Third, the evaluation protocols differ. The new model used rolling-origin backtesting, an appropriate method for time-series evaluation. The baseline was taken from a production dashboard with an unspecified evaluation methodology. These are not equivalent comparisons.

For a valid comparison, the ARIMA baseline must be re-evaluated on the same corrected dataset, at the seven-day planning horizon, using rolling-origin backtesting. Until that comparison exists, the 7% figure cannot support a replacement recommendation. The phased rollout is sensible in principle but should explicitly include a live A/B comparison that generates equivalent metrics for both systems.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Data targets differ — raw sales vs. corrected demand
Issue 2: Forecast horizons incompatible — 1-day vs. 7-day aggregate
Issue 3: Evaluation protocols differ — unspecified dashboard vs. rolling-origin backtesting
